topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a sharp little jolt against the otherwise placid hum of my afternoon. I glanced down, my thumb hovering over the screen, and froze. It was Professor Albright’s profile picture, the same slightly stern, yet surprisingly warm, headshot I’d seen on the syllabus. My breath hitched, a sudden lightness bubbling up in my chest. I navigated to his feed, expecting academic updates, but instead, I saw photos of him hiking in the mountains and even a slightly blurry picture of him laughing with a group of students. A slow, wide smile spread across my face, tugging at my cheeks. I quickly scrolled through a few more posts, a feeling of pure, uncontainable buoyancy making my limbs feel light and ready to spring into action. This was… unexpected, and entirely wonderful.

[story 2]

I was deep into a brainstorming session, the whiteboard covered in chaotic diagrams, when my phone vibrated insistently on the desk. It was a direct message from Professor Davies. My heart gave a surprising little thump against my ribs. I picked up the device, and there, in a recent post, was a candid shot of him at a local market, looking completely relaxed. A wave of warmth washed over me, chasing away the usual afternoon slump. I leaned back in my chair, the tension that had been coiled in my shoulders finally dissolving. I started tapping away on my keyboard, my fingers moving with a newfound speed and purpose. Everything suddenly seemed brighter, clearer, as if a switch had been flipped inside me.

[story 3]

It happened while I was waiting for the library to open, fiddling with my phone, hoping for some distraction. A notification popped up: Professor Chen had just shared a short video clip of his cat attempting to catch a laser pointer. I couldn't help the involuntary grin that stretched across my face. It felt like a sudden surge of vitality, a feeling of being perfectly aligned with the world. I didn't just look at the video; I started typing a reply, my thoughts racing ahead of the actual words. The usual dull ache of studying seemed to vanish, replaced by a vibrant, almost buzzing readiness. I felt an immediate need to go find something else to tackle, something exciting to dive into.
